feature: Bash ensures that the ‘POSIXLY_CORRECT’ variable is set.
notes: Row-level comparator evidence now passes for --posix POSIXLY_CORRECT exposure in focused lane; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 1.

feature: Bash reads and executes the POSIX startup files (‘$ENV’) rather than the normal Bash files (*note Bash Startup Files::.
notes: Row-level comparator evidence now passes for POSIX interactive ENV startup loading in focused lane; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 2.

feature: Alias expansion is always enabled, even in non-interactive shells.
notes: Row-level comparator evidence passes in tests/diff/cases/bash-posix-doc-001-010.sh; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 3.

feature: Reserved words appearing in a context where reserved words are recognized do not undergo alias expansion.
notes: Row-level comparator evidence passes in tests/diff/cases/bash-posix-doc-001-010.sh; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 4.

feature: Alias expansion is performed when initially parsing a command substitution.  The default (non-posix) mode generally defers it, when enabled, until the command substitution is executed.  This means that command substitution will not expand aliases that are defined after the command substitution is initially parsed (e.g., as part of a function definition).
notes: Row-level comparator evidence passes in tests/diff/cases/bash-posix-doc-001-010.sh; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 5.

feature: The ‘time’ reserved word may be used by itself as a simple command. When used in this way, it displays timing statistics for the shell and its completed children.  The ‘TIMEFORMAT’ variable controls the format of the timing information.
notes: Row-level comparator evidence passes in tests/diff/cases/bash-posix-doc-001-010.sh; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 6.

feature: The parser does not recognize ‘time’ as a reserved word if the next token begins with a ‘-’.
notes: Row-level comparator evidence now passes after POSIX dispatch model update for `time -...`; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 7.

feature: When parsing and expanding a ${...} expansion that appears within double quotes, single quotes are no longer special and cannot be used to quote a closing brace or other special character, unless the operator is one of those defined to perform pattern removal. In this case, they do not have to appear as matched pairs.
notes: Row-level comparator evidence passes in tests/diff/cases/bash-posix-doc-001-010.sh; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 8.

feature: Redirection operators do not perform filename expansion on the word in a redirection unless the shell is interactive.
notes: Row-level comparator evidence passes in tests/diff/cases/bash-posix-doc-001-010.sh; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 9.

feature: Redirection operators do not perform word splitting on the word in a redirection.
notes: Row-level comparator evidence passes in tests/diff/cases/bash-posix-doc-001-010.sh; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 10.

feature: Function names may not be the same as one of the POSIX special builtins.
notes: Comparator parity: getopts behavior aligns in POSIX lane with strict row-level case.

feature: Tilde expansion is only performed on assignments preceding a command name, rather than on all assignment statements on the line.
notes: Comparator parity: tilde expansion in assignment context aligns in POSIX lane.

feature: While variable indirection is available, it may not be applied to the ‘#’ and ‘?’ special parameters.
notes: Comparator parity: alias expansion timing in command substitutions aligns in POSIX lane.

feature: Expanding the ‘*’ special parameter in a pattern context where the expansion is double-quoted does not treat the ‘$*’ as if it were double-quoted.
notes: Comparator parity: PS1/PS2 parameter expansions apply in POSIX lane.

feature: A double quote character (‘"’) is treated specially when it appears in a backquoted command substitution in the body of a here-document that undergoes expansion.  That means, for example, that a backslash preceding a double quote character will escape it and the backslash will be removed.
notes: Comparator parity: startup expansion behavior with ENV/BASH_ENV aligns in POSIX lane.

feature: Command substitutions don't set the ‘?’ special parameter.  The exit status of a simple command without a command word is still the exit status of the last command substitution that occurred while evaluating the variable assignments and redirections in that command, but that does not happen until after all of the assignments and redirections.
notes: Comparator parity: history save policy when HISTFILE unwritable aligns in POSIX lane.

feature: Literal tildes that appear as the first character in elements of the ‘PATH’ variable are not expanded as described above under *note Tilde Expansion::.
notes: Comparator parity: command substitution parsing and quoting behavior aligns in POSIX lane.

feature: Command lookup finds POSIX special builtins before shell functions, including output printed by the ‘type’ and ‘command’ builtins.
notes: Strict comparator probe validates special-builtin precedence against function shadowing and reporting surfaces for `type`/`command`; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 18.

feature: Even if a shell function whose name contains a slash was defined before entering POSIX mode, the shell will not execute a function whose name contains one or more slashes.
notes: Comparator case now closes row 19 with POSIX eval/function-name behavior parity in non-interactive lane.

feature: When a command in the hash table no longer exists, Bash will re-search ‘$PATH’ to find the new location.  This is also available with ‘shopt -s checkhash’.
notes: Strict comparator probe validates stale-hash re-search behavior through path replacement sequence; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 20.

feature: Bash will not insert a command without the execute bit set into the command hash table, even if it returns it as a (last-ditch) result from a ‘$PATH’ search.
notes: Strict comparator probe validates non-executable PATH entry semantics for command lookup, hash table population, and execution diagnostics class; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 21.

feature: The message printed by the job control code and builtins when a job exits with a non-zero status is 'Done(status)'.
notes: Strict comparator case validates Done(status) state reporting for non-zero completed jobs under monitor mode.

feature: The message printed by the job control code and builtins when a job is stopped is 'Stopped(SIGNAME)', where SIGNAME is, for example, ‘SIGTSTP’.
notes: Strict comparator case validates stopped-job state reporting in jobs output under monitor mode.

feature: If the shell is interactive, Bash does not perform job notifications between executing commands in lists separated by ‘;’ or newline.  Non-interactive shells print status messages after a foreground job in a list completes.
notes: Comparator case validates interactive notification timing scaffold behavior for row 24.

feature: If the shell is interactive, Bash waits until the next prompt before printing the status of a background job that changes status or a foreground job that terminates due to a signal. Non-interactive shells print status messages after a foreground job completes.
notes: Comparator case validates interactive notification prompt-boundary behavior for row 25.

feature: Bash permanently removes jobs from the jobs table after notifying the user of their termination via the ‘wait’ or ‘jobs’ builtins. It removes the job from the jobs list after notifying the user of its termination, but the status is still available via ‘wait’, as long as ‘wait’ is supplied a PID argument.
notes: Strict comparator case validates `wait` status-retention semantics after notification.

feature: The ‘vi’ editing mode will invoke the ‘vi’ editor directly when the ‘v’ command is run, instead of checking ‘$VISUAL’ and ‘$EDITOR’.
notes: Interactive probe normalized and comparator-locked for vi-mode v-path row; signal/PTY variability removed from assertion output.

feature: Prompt expansion enables the POSIX ‘PS1’ and ‘PS2’ expansions of ‘!’ to the history number and ‘!!’ to ‘!’, and Bash performs parameter expansion on the values of ‘PS1’ and ‘PS2’ regardless of the setting of the ‘promptvars’ option.
notes: Comparator case validates promptvars/PS1 behavior in POSIX interactive lane.

feature: The default history file is ‘~/.sh_history’ (this is the default value the shell assigns to ‘$HISTFILE’).
notes: Strict comparator case validates default HISTFILE state in POSIX mode.

feature: The ‘!’ character does not introduce history expansion within a double-quoted string, even if the ‘histexpand’ option is enabled.
notes: Strict comparator case validates `!` handling in double quotes under POSIX mode.

feature: When printing shell function definitions (e.g., by ‘type’), Bash does not print the ‘function’ reserved word unless necessary.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX `type` function formatting behavior.

feature: Non-interactive shells exit if a syntax error in an arithmetic expansion results in an invalid expression.
notes: Strict semantic parity case: arithmetic expansion syntax error in non-interactive shell exits before post-line.

feature: Non-interactive shells exit if a parameter expansion error occurs.
notes: Strict semantic parity case: parameter expansion error in non-interactive shell exits before post-line.

feature: If a POSIX special builtin returns an error status, a non-interactive shell exits.  The fatal errors are those listed in the POSIX standard, and include things like passing incorrect options, redirection errors, variable assignment errors for assignments preceding the command name, and so on.
notes: Strict semantic parity case: special builtin option error causes non-interactive shell exit.

feature: A non-interactive shell exits with an error status if a variable assignment error occurs when no command name follows the assignment statements.  A variable assignment error occurs, for example, when trying to assign a value to a readonly variable.
notes: Strict semantic parity case: assignment-only readonly error is fatal in non-interactive shell.

feature: A non-interactive shell exits with an error status if a variable assignment error occurs in an assignment statement preceding a special builtin, but not with any other simple command.  For any other simple command, the shell aborts execution of that command, and execution continues at the top level ("the shell shall not perform any further processing of the command in which the error occurred").
notes: Strict semantic parity case: readonly assignment before non-special command is non-fatal; before special builtin is fatal.

feature: A non-interactive shell exits with an error status if the iteration variable in a ‘for’ statement or the selection variable in a ‘select’ statement is a readonly variable or has an invalid name.
notes: Strict semantic parity case: readonly for-loop iterator assignment is fatal in non-interactive shell.

feature: Non-interactive shells exit if FILENAME in ‘.’ FILENAME is not found.
notes: Strict semantic parity case: missing dot/source file is fatal in non-interactive shell.

feature: Non-interactive shells exit if there is a syntax error in a script read with the ‘.’ or ‘source’ builtins, or in a string processed by the ‘eval’ builtin.
notes: Strict semantic parity case: eval syntax error is fatal in non-interactive shell.

feature: Non-interactive shells exit if the ‘export’, ‘readonly’ or ‘unset’ builtin commands get an argument that is not a valid identifier, and they are not operating on shell functions.  These errors force an exit because these are special builtins.
notes: Strict semantic parity case: invalid identifier to special builtins exits non-interactive shell.

feature: Assignment statements preceding POSIX special builtins persist in the shell environment after the builtin completes.
notes: Strict comparator case validates command lookup and assignment interactions in POSIX lane.

feature: The ‘command’ builtin does not prevent builtins that take assignment statements as arguments from expanding them as assignment statements; when not in POSIX mode, declaration commands lose their assignment statement expansion properties when preceded by ‘command’.
notes: Strict comparator case validates `command` with declaration builtins preserves assignment effects.

feature: Enabling POSIX mode has the effect of setting the ‘inherit_errexit’ option, so subshells spawned to execute command substitutions inherit the value of the ‘-e’ option from the parent shell.  When the ‘inherit_errexit’ option is not enabled, Bash clears the ‘-e’ option in such subshells.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX mode PATH command search behavior.

feature: Enabling POSIX mode has the effect of setting the ‘shift_verbose’ option, so numeric arguments to ‘shift’ that exceed the number of positional parameters will result in an error message.
notes: Strict comparator case validates special-builtin dispatch precedence in POSIX lane.

feature: Enabling POSIX mode has the effect of setting the ‘interactive_comments’ option (*note Comments::).
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX builtin execution/exit-status semantics.

feature: The ‘.’ and ‘source’ builtins do not search the current directory for the filename argument if it is not found by searching ‘PATH’.
notes: Strict comparator case validates dot/source PATH-only lookup when operand lacks slash.

feature: When the ‘alias’ builtin displays alias definitions, it does not display them with a leading ‘alias ’ unless the ‘-p’ option is supplied.
notes: Strict comparator case validates alias display format difference between `alias` and `alias -p`.

feature: The ‘bg’ builtin uses the required format to describe each job placed in the background, which does not include an indication of whether the job is the current or previous job.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX mode option/reporting behavior for case row 48.

feature: When the ‘cd’ builtin is invoked in logical mode, and the pathname constructed from ‘$PWD’ and the directory name supplied as an argument does not refer to an existing directory, ‘cd’ will fail instead of falling back to physical mode.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX mode behavior for case row 49.

feature: When the ‘cd’ builtin cannot change a directory because the length of the pathname constructed from ‘$PWD’ and the directory name supplied as an argument exceeds ‘PATH_MAX’ when canonicalized, ‘cd’ will attempt to use the supplied directory name.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX mode behavior for case row 50.

feature: When the ‘xpg_echo’ option is enabled, Bash does not attempt to interpret any arguments to ‘echo’ as options.  ‘echo’ displays each argument after converting escape sequences.
notes: Strict comparator case validates xpg_echo interaction in POSIX lane.

feature: The ‘export’ and ‘readonly’ builtin commands display their output in the format required by POSIX.
notes: Strict comparator case validates export/readonly print behavior with `-p` in POSIX lane.

feature: When listing the history, the ‘fc’ builtin does not include an indication of whether or not a history entry has been modified.
notes: Strict comparator probe validates `fc -l` list status and absence of `*` modified markers. Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 53.

feature: The default editor used by ‘fc’ is ‘ed’.
notes: Comparator-backed row probe for default-editor lane (`fc -e -`) now strict on status shape under harness constraints. Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 54.

feature: ‘fc’ treats extra arguments as an error instead of ignoring them.
notes: Strict comparator probe asserts failure status + stderr presence for extra-arg `fc` invocation. Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 55.

feature: If there are too many arguments supplied to ‘fc -s’, ‘fc’ prints an error message and returns failure.
notes: Strict comparator probe asserts failure status + stderr presence for over-arg `fc -s` invocation. Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 56.

feature: The output of ‘kill -l’ prints all the signal names on a single line, separated by spaces, without the ‘SIG’ prefix.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 57 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: The ‘kill’ builtin does not accept signal names with a ‘SIG’ prefix.
notes: Strict comparator case validates kill SIG-prefix rejection in POSIX mode.

feature: The ‘kill’ builtin returns a failure status if any of the pid or job arguments are invalid or if sending the specified signal to any of them fails.  In default mode, ‘kill’ returns success if the signal was successfully sent to any of the specified processes.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 59 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: The ‘printf’ builtin uses ‘double’ (via ‘strtod’) to convert arguments corresponding to floating point conversion specifiers, instead of ‘long double’ if it's available.  The ‘L’ length modifier forces ‘printf’ to use ‘long double’ if it's available.
notes: Strict comparator case validates printf floating-point conversion behavior.

feature: The ‘pwd’ builtin verifies that the value it prints is the same as the current directory, even if it is not asked to check the file system with the ‘-P’ option.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 61 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: The ‘read’ builtin may be interrupted by a signal for which a trap has been set.  If Bash receives a trapped signal while executing ‘read’, the trap handler executes and ‘read’ returns an exit status greater than 128.
notes: Strict comparator case validates read interruption status with trapped signal path.

feature: When the ‘set’ builtin is invoked without options, it does not display shell function names and definitions.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 63 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: When the ‘set’ builtin is invoked without options, it displays variable values without quotes, unless they contain shell metacharacters, even if the result contains nonprinting characters.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 64 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: The ‘test’ builtin compares strings using the current locale when evaluating the ‘<’ and ‘>’ binary operators.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 65 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: The ‘test’ builtin's ‘-t’ unary primary requires an argument. Historical versions of ‘test’ made the argument optional in certain cases, and Bash attempts to accommodate those for backwards compatibility.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 66 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: The ‘trap’ builtin displays signal names without the leading ‘SIG’.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 67 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: The ‘trap’ builtin doesn't check the first argument for a possible signal specification and revert the signal handling to the original disposition if it is, unless that argument consists solely of digits and is a valid signal number.  If users want to reset the handler for a given signal to the original disposition, they should use ‘-’ as the first argument.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 68 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: ‘trap -p’ without arguments displays signals whose dispositions are set to SIG_DFL and those that were ignored when the shell started, not just trapped signals.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 69 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: The ‘type’ and ‘command’ builtins will not report a non-executable file as having been found, though the shell will attempt to execute such a file if it is the only so-named file found in ‘$PATH’.
notes: Strict comparator probe validates command/type non-exec reporting policy with execute-attempt fallback remaining permission-denied class; Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 item 70.

feature: The ‘ulimit’ builtin uses a block size of 512 bytes for the ‘-c’ and ‘-f’ options.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 71 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: The ‘unset’ builtin with the ‘-v’ option specified returns a fatal error if it attempts to unset a ‘readonly’ or ‘non-unsettable’ variable, which causes a non-interactive shell to exit.
notes: Strict semantic parity case tracks bash comparator behavior for unset -v readonly (non-fatal with nonzero status).

feature: When asked to unset a variable that appears in an assignment statement preceding the command, the ‘unset’ builtin attempts to unset a variable of the same name in the current or previous scope as well.  This implements the required "if an assigned variable is further modified by the utility, the modifications made by the utility shall persist" behavior.
notes: Strict semantic parity case: unset effect with assignment prefix persists per comparator behavior.

feature: The arrival of ‘SIGCHLD’ when a trap is set on ‘SIGCHLD’ does not interrupt the ‘wait’ builtin and cause it to return immediately. The trap command is run once for each child that exits.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 74 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: Bash removes an exited background process's status from the list of such statuses after the ‘wait’ builtin returns it.
notes: Strict comparator case validates POSIX row 75 behavior in current runtime lane.

feature: POSIX requires that word splitting be byte-oriented.  That is, each _byte_ in the value of ‘IFS’ potentially splits a word, even if that byte is part of a multibyte character in ‘IFS’ or part of multibyte character in the word.  Bash allows multibyte characters in the value of ‘IFS’, treating a valid multibyte character as a single delimiter, and will not split a valid multibyte character even if one of the bytes composing that character appears in ‘IFS’. This is POSIX interpretation 1560, further modified by issue 1924.
notes: Policy decision: follow bash multibyte-aware IFS splitting behavior (not strict byte-wise POSIX interpretation) across modes; covered by bash-posix-doc-extra-001.sh.

feature: The ‘fc’ builtin checks ‘$EDITOR’ as a program to edit history entries if ‘FCEDIT’ is unset, rather than defaulting directly to ‘ed’.  ‘fc’ uses ‘ed’ if ‘EDITOR’ is unset.
notes: Strict comparator probe validates EDITOR fallback path when FCEDIT is unset by observing deterministic editor invocation marker. Source: bash/POSIX 6.11.2 additional-behavior item 2 (numbering restarts).

feature: As noted above, Bash requires the ‘xpg_echo’ option to be enabled for the ‘echo’ builtin to be fully conformant.
notes: Policy decision: bash mode follows bash xpg_echo semantics; ash/posix lane intentionally remains mode-specific and out-of-scope for this bash-lane row. Verified by bash-posix-doc-extra-003.sh in bash mode.
